narrative:
First Warn Forecast
Friday, May 15, 2026
First Warn Headlines
Storm chances continue through the weekend with isolated strong to severe thunderstorms possible at times.
Increasing concern for a more organized severe weather setup Monday through Wednesday across Missouri and Illinois.
Hotter, more humid, and summer-like conditions expected through early next week before cooler air returns later next week.

Warm, humid, and increasingly unsettled weather is settling into the region as we head into the weekend and early next week. While isolated strong storms are possible at times through the weekend, a more organized severe weather threat may develop sometime between Monday and Wednesday as a stronger cold front approaches the Midwest.

For now, today will feature a mix of clouds and sunshine with temperatures climbing into the middle and upper 80s across much of the St. Louis Metro, Central Missouri, Southeast Missouri, and Southern Illinois. Humidity levels will continue to rise, making it feel noticeably more summer-like across the region. Early morning thunderstorms across portions of Missouri and Illinois may linger through the morning hours, especially north and east of St. Louis, before gradually weakening.

There remains a low-end chance for isolated thunderstorms later this afternoon and evening, especially if leftover outflow boundaries from the morning storms can act as a trigger for redevelopment. Any storm that forms could produce hail and gusty winds, but widespread severe weather is not expected today. Still, this time of year only takes one storm to become “that guy” at the backyard barbecue. Midwest weather loves a plot twist.

Tonight into Saturday morning, another round of thunderstorms may develop upstream across Iowa and Nebraska before tracking southeastward toward Missouri and Illinois. Some storms could contain locally heavy rainfall, lightning, hail, and isolated damaging wind gusts. Confidence in exact timing and coverage remains somewhat low because several small-scale disturbances are moving through nearly zonal flow aloft, making storm placement difficult to pin down more than several hours in advance.

By Sunday, much of the region may briefly find itself in a warmer and somewhat quieter stretch of weather. Temperatures could push into the upper 80s and even near 90 degrees in a few locations with increasing sunshine and gusty south winds. It will certainly feel like late June instead of mid-May.

Attention then turns to Monday through Wednesday, where confidence is increasing that a stronger storm system and cold front will move into the Midwest. The exact timing of this front remains uncertain, and that timing will ultimately determine the magnitude of the severe weather threat across Missouri and Illinois.

Here is what we know right now:

Multiple rounds of showers and thunderstorms appear likely Monday into Wednesday.
The atmosphere will become increasingly warm, humid, and unstable.
Strong wind shear will likely be present ahead of the approaching front.
If storms arrive during peak daytime heating, the risk for severe thunderstorms would increase substantially.
All severe weather hazards may eventually come into play, including damaging winds, large hail, torrential rainfall, and isolated tornadoes.

Forecast confidence on exact storm timing is still moderate to low, but the overall pattern certainly supports the potential for active weather next week. Residents across the St. Louis Metro, Central Missouri, Southeast Missouri, and Southern Illinois should closely monitor forecast updates through the weekend as details become clearer.

The good news? Once the front moves through, cooler and somewhat less humid air is expected later next week with temperatures dropping back closer to seasonal normals. Until then, keep the umbrella nearby, keep the weather apps charged, and maybe don’t tempt fate by saying, “It doesn’t look that bad outside.” Famous last words in Missouri weather.
